text: The surgeon reflects the large and small bowel away from the kidney, exposing the posterior abdominal structures. The inferior vena cava (IVC) and the renal hilum, containing a single vein and two arteries, are dissected and clearly identified.
The surgeon identifies the kidney beneath layers of surrounding fat and locates the tumor within the kidney using ultrasound for precise confirmation.
The surgeon excises the kidney tumor, ensuring a clear margin is present around the excised tissue to retrieve all cancerous cells.

The surgeon closes the defect left by the removed tumor, ensuring the urinary system entry is securely sutured. Major blood vessels are oversewn to prevent bleeding upon restoring blood flow to the kidney.
The surgeon removes the clamps from the kidney arteries, visibly restoring blood flow to the kidney after a 16-minute period of ischemia.

The surgeon applies large bolstering sutures to further close the defect, ensuring no significant bleeding is visible. Blood clotting products are used to achieve hemostasis, and bolster padding is tightly secured to compress the defect.

The surgical specimen is retrieved, showing no visible cancer on any surface. The layers are repositioned, and the kidney is restored to its original position in the posterior part behind the abdominal cavity.
